---
title: "Essay: Understanding Shinylive"
---

## What is Shinylive?

Shinylive is a way to run Shiny apps entirely in a web browser, without needing a dedicated R server running in the background. It works by compiling R (or Python) into WebAssembly, a technology that lets code run directly inside the browser itself. In simple terms: the "server" logic that used to live on a remote machine now runs right on the visitor's own computer, inside their browser tab.

## How It Differs From Traditional Shiny

|   | Traditional Shiny | Shinylive |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Where it runs** | On a server (needs to stay running) | In the user's browser (no server needed) |
| **Hosting** | Requires a server or service like shinyapps.io | Can be hosted as static files (e.g., GitHub Pages) |
| **Cost** | Ongoing server costs | Free to host as static files |
| **Startup time** | Usually fast | Slower first load (browser downloads R/Python runtime) |
| **Package support** | Full access to any R package | Limited to packages compatible with WebAssembly |
| **Scalability** | Server can get overwhelmed with many users | Scales naturally since each user runs their own copy |

## Pros of Shinylive

-   **No server required** — eliminates hosting costs and maintenance
-   **Easy to share** — can be hosted anywhere static files are supported, including GitHub Pages
-   **Scales effortlessly** — since each visitor's browser does the work, there's no risk of the app slowing down under heavy traffic

## Cons of Shinylive

-   **Slower initial load** — the browser has to download the R (or Python) runtime environment before the app starts
-   **Limited package support** — not every R package works in this browser-based environment yet
-   **Less suited for heavy computation** — since it runs on the visitor's device, very data-intensive apps may perform worse than on a dedicated server

## When to Use Each

Traditional Shiny is still the better choice for apps that need heavy computation, access to a database, or use packages not yet supported in WebAssembly. Shinylive shines for lightweight, shareable tools you want to distribute freely without worrying about hosting infrastructure — a perfect fit for a public-facing demo or a free educational tool.
